title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Study Objective and Data Scope
Objective: identify visual and statistical patterns that explain poor profitability and high volatility.



Area
Summary




Dataset
Spanish motor vehicle insurance portfolio


Original data
354,140 policy records, 47 variables


Analytical data
352,338 records after missing value treatment


Period
2022 to 2024


Key outcomes
Loss ratio, claim frequency, has claim



Three derived measures drive the analysis:

Loss ratio = total incurred ÷ total premium — a ratio above 1 means the insurer paid out more than it collected
Claim frequency = total claims ÷ total exposure — higher values indicate a more volatile portfolio
Has claim = 1 if any claim was filed, 0 otherwise — separates claiming from non-claiming policies


The analysis is based on a large real-world insurance portfolio covering 2022 to 2024. After cleaning, only 1,802 records were removed, so the final sample remains highly representative. Loss ratio answers the profitability question, are we collecting enough premium to cover claims? Claim frequency answers the volatility question, how often are claims occurring? Has claim allows us to isolate the 14.3% of policies that drive all portfolio losses.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Portfolio Profile


Third-Party Combined (58%) dominates, performance issues here affect the whole portfolio
96.5% of policyholders have a Good bonus score, portfolio appears low risk on the surface
Aggregate stability can mask concentrated pockets of high-risk segments


The portfolio is heavily concentrated in Third-Party Combined policies at 58%, with Comprehensive with Excess second at 25.6%. Basic Third-Party is only 1.6% so patterns in this group must be interpreted carefully. The high proportion of Good bonus score policyholders creates a misleading picture of low overall risk, the real losses are concentrated in a small number of higher-risk segments that are not visible at the aggregate level.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Claim Occurrence and Severity


85.7% of policies record no claims but the 14.3% with claims drive all losses
Most claims cost below €2,500 — but occasional large losses have an outsized impact
The average claim cost (€1,271.98) is well above the typical claim (€898.68) a sign that severe claims are pulling the average up


Most policies do not generate claims. The 85.7% no-claim rate makes the portfolio look calm at a high level, but the smaller claiming group is responsible for all incurred costs. Most claims are relatively affordable below €2,500, but a small number of expensive claims pull the average well above the typical value. This pattern means the portfolio’s profitability is more sensitive to large individual losses than to the volume of claims.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Profitability Patterns: Loss Ratio


Most claiming policies record a loss ratio between 1.0 and 2.5, incurred costs regularly exceed premiums
The average loss ratio (2.58) is noticeably higher than the typical value (2.0), a small number of very costly policies are pulling it up
The long right tail signals that catastrophic losses, have a significant impact on overall profitability


Among policies that have made a claim, the loss ratio is concentrated between 1 and 2.5 meaning the insurer typically pays out one to two and a half times what it collected in premiums. The average is higher than the typical value because a small number of policies with extreme losses skew the result upward. These catastrophic cases are relatively rare but disproportionately affect portfolio performance.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Volatility Patterns: Claim Frequency


Most claiming policies make around 1 claim per year but a small group claims far more often
The average claim frequency (1.95) is higher than the typical value (1.28) frequent claimants distort the picture
Portfolio volatility is driven by this small group of repeat claimants, not the majority


Among claiming policies, most make one claim per year. However a small group of policyholders claims much more frequently, pulling the average well above the typical value. This means that portfolio volatility is not spread evenly, it is concentrated in a minority of high-frequency claimants. Identifying and managing this group is key to stabilising the portfolio.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Portfolio Loss Ratio Trend


Loss ratio has been rising steadily, 0.657 (2022) → 0.665 (2023) → 0.747 (2024)
The portfolio is still profitable, but the gap to break-even is narrowing
Claims costs are growing faster than premiums, action is needed before the trend continues


The portfolio remains profitable across all three years, but the direction of travel is concerning. The loss ratio barely moved between 2022 and 2023 but jumped noticeably in 2024. If this pace of deterioration continues, the portfolio could reach break-even within a few years. This trend is what motivates the deeper analysis into which segments and risk factors are driving the losses.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Key Loss Drivers: Profitability


Policy type is the strongest driver of portfolio profitability
Basic Third-Party policies are the least profitable — claims cost over 4 times the premium collected
Comprehensive with Excess is the most stable — policyholders absorb minor costs before claiming, keeping losses predictable


Policy type is the clearest differentiator of profitability. Basic Third-Party has the highest typical loss ratio, meaning claims far exceed premiums for this group. However its small size in the portfolio means the overall impact is limited. Comprehensive with Excess performs best because the excess structure discourages policyholders from filing small claims, so only more significant losses get reported. This keeps the loss ratio lower and more predictable.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Key Loss Drivers: Volatility


Bonus score is the strongest driver of portfolio volatility
Policyholders with a Bad bonus score claim nearly twice as often as those with a Good score
Past claims history is a reliable signal of future claim behaviour


Bonus score reflects a policyholder’s claims history and is the clearest indicator of future volatility. Those with a Bad score claim nearly twice as frequently as Good score policyholders. This confirms that past behaviour is a strong predictor of future behaviour in motor insurance. The portfolio appears low risk overall because 96.5% of policyholders have a Good score, but the Bad and Neutral groups contribute disproportionately to overall claim frequency.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Risk Concentration: Severity vs Frequency


Comprehensive without Excess — claims often and claims expensively, but generates little premium, this is the most loss-making segment
Third-Party Combined — claims very often and carries the largest premium volume anD the biggest frequency risk in the portfolio
Comprehensive with Excess — claims less often, generates strong premium and the healthiest segment


The bubble chart puts three dimensions together. How often a segment claims, how expensive those claims are, and how much premium it generates. Comprehensive without Excess is the most financially exposed as it sits in the worst position on both dimensions while contributing relatively little premium. Third-Party Combined dominates by size but its risk is driven by frequency rather than severity. Comprehensive with Excess is the most sustainable segment.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Executive Implications

The 14.3% of policies that claim are responsible for all portfolio losses, while the majority generate no cost at all. The loss ratio has risen 13.7% in three years and is steadily approaching break-even.
Policy type is the clearest driver of profitability. Third-Party products consistently pay out more than they collect.
Bonus score is the clearest driver of volatility. Policyholders with a poor claims history claim nearly twice as often as those with a good one. - Comprehensive without Excess is the most exposed segment, combining frequent and expensive claims with insufficient premium to cover them.


The overall portfolio looks calm but the underlying dynamics are moving in the wrong direction. The year-on-year deterioration in loss ratio is driven by a small number of high-risk segments, particularly Comprehensive without Excess and Third-Party based products. Bonus score is the strongest signal for future volatility. Addressing these segments through pricing, underwriting and product design should be the immediate priority.

title: Executive Summary: Motor Vehicle Insurance Loss Drivers
text:
Recommendations and Next Steps

Review pricing adequacy for Third-Party based policies
Monitor Bad and Neutral bonus score groups more closely
Investigate Comprehensive without Excess for small claim filing behaviour
Apply regression modelling to confirm and quantify exploratory findings
Enrich portfolio with traffic density, weather and regional accident data


The recommendations translate the visual findings into action. Pricing needs to be reviewed for Third-Party products given their poor loss ratios. Bad and Neutral bonus score policyholders should be monitored more closely as they claim more frequently. Comprehensive without Excess needs investigation as removing the excess removes the financial disincentive to file small claims. Future analysis should move beyond exploration into confirmatory modelling and incorporate richer external data.

title: In-Class Exercise 6: Designing Web-enabled Visual Analytics Application with Shiny
text: In this in-class exercise, we explored Shiny, R’s framework for building interactive, web-enabled visual analytics applications. Unlike static ggplot2 visualisations, Shiny allows users to interact with data dynamically through widgets such as sliders and dropdowns — without needing to write any JavaScript or HTML.

title: In-Class Exercise 6: Designing Web-enabled Visual Analytics Application with Shiny
text: In this in-class exercise, we explored Shiny, R’s framework for building interactive, web-enabled visual analytics applications. Unlike static ggplot2 visualisations, Shiny allows users to interact with data dynamically through widgets such as sliders and dropdowns — without needing to write any JavaScript or HTML.

title: In-Class Exercise 6: Designing Web-enabled Visual Analytics Application with Shiny
text:
Reflections
Building the Shiny app helped me understand the clear separation between the UI (what the user sees) and the server (the logic behind what gets displayed). What clicked for me was how reactivity works — outputs don’t just run once, they automatically re-execute whenever a relevant input changes. This is a fundamentally different mental model from writing a standard R script top to bottom.
I also noticed a practical difference between aes() and aes_string() — when the variable name comes from a reactive input (a string), aes_string() is needed so ggplot2 can interpret it correctly. Small details like this matter a lot when inputs are dynamic.

title: In-Class Exercise 6: Designing Web-enabled Visual Analytics Application with Shiny
text:
Key Concepts

Shiny Building Blocks
Every Shiny app has two components:

UI (User Interface) — defines the layout and widgets the user interacts with
Server — contains the R logic that generates outputs in response to inputs



Reactivity
Shiny’s reactivity system ensures outputs update automatically when inputs change. The key reactive functions are:







Function
Purpose




reactive()
Creates a reactive expression that caches its result


observe()
Runs code for side effects when inputs change


eventReactive()
Only reacts when a specific event occurs (e.g. button click)


isolate()
Reads a reactive value without triggering re-execution

title: In-Class Exercise 6: Designing Web-enabled Visual Analytics Application with Shiny
text:
Pupils Exam Results Dashboard

Loading Packages and Data

pacman::p_load(shiny, tidyverse)
exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



UI
The UI uses sidebarLayout() with two widgets — a selectInput() for subject selection and a sliderInput() for controlling the number of histogram bins.

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Pupils Exam Results Dashboard"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput(inputId = "variable",
                  label = "Subject:",
                  choices = c("English" = "ENGLISH",
                              "Maths"   = "MATHS",
                              "Science" = "SCIENCE"),
                  selected = "ENGLISH"),
      sliderInput(inputId = "bins",
                  label = "Number of Bins",
                  min   = 5,
                  max   = 20,
                  value = 10)
    ),
    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("distPlot")
    )
  )
)



Server
The server uses aes_string() to pass the reactive input$variable (a string) into ggplot2.

server <- function(input, output) {
  output$distPlot <- renderPlot({
    ggplot(exam,
           aes_string(x = input$variable)) +
      geom_histogram(bins  = input$bins,
                     color = "black",
                     fill  = "light blue")
  })
}



Running the App

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text: In-Class Exercise 4 consists of two Tableau dashboards. One exploring English score distributions by class, and another analysing Food Service KPI ratings using diverging stacked bar charts.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text: In-Class Exercise 4 consists of two Tableau dashboards. One exploring English score distributions by class, and another analysing Food Service KPI ratings using diverging stacked bar charts.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
section: Dashboard 1 — English Score Distribution by Class
text:
Dashboard 1 — English Score Distribution by Class
This dashboard shows a boxplot of English scores by class alongside a histogram of the overall score distribution. The average English score across all students is 67.18.



English Score Distribution by Class


From the Exam Data, I noticed that 3D performed better than 3C in the English exam, as 3D has a higher overall score distribution. This shows that the boxplot is useful because it allows me to compare not only the general performance between classes, but also the spread and consistency of scores within each class.
The boxplot also helps reduce the zone of irrelevancy. In a bar chart, the viewer may focus too much on small differences between bars that may not be meaningful, making minor gaps look more important than they really are. With a boxplot, I can see whether the scores actually overlap, whether the difference between classes is meaningful, and whether the overall distribution supports the conclusion. This makes the boxplot more useful for comparing exam performance across classes.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text:
Dashboard 2 — Food Service KPI Report
This dashboard uses diverging stacked bar charts to compare food service satisfaction ratings across different wards and months (October and November).



Food Service KPI Report


For the Food Service Survey Data, the dashboards help summarise many responses in a clear and organised way. The charts made it easier to compare satisfaction levels across different food service items, months and wards. I could quickly see which areas received more positive ratings and which areas may need improvement.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text:
Reflections
Overall, I learnt that visual analytics is useful because it turns raw data into insights that are easier to understand, explain and use for decision making. Different chart types serve different purposes — boxplots are better for comparing distributions across groups, while diverging stacked bar charts work well for summarising Likert-scale survey data across multiple categories.

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text: Network data consists of two key components — nodes (entities) and edges (relationships between entities). In this exercise, email correspondence data from GAStech, an oil exploration company, is used to explore and visualise communication patterns between 55 employees over two weeks.

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text: Network data consists of two key components — nodes (entities) and edges (relationships between entities). In this exercise, email correspondence data from GAStech, an oil exploration company, is used to explore and visualise communication patterns between 55 employees over two weeks.

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text:
Reflections
Network visualisation uses nodes(data points) and edges (links) to visually map connected data, it reveals patterns that tabular data cannot such as which employees are central connectors, which departments communicate most, and whether communication clusters form around certain groups. The key takeaway is that tidygraph makes network data manipulation feel familiar using dplyr verbs, while ggraph extends ggplot2 grammar to network graphs. The interactive visNetwork output added practical value by allowing exploration of individual nodes and edges dynamically.

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(igraph, tidygraph, ggraph,
               visNetwork, lubridate, clock,
               tidyverse, graphlayouts,
               concaveman, ggforce)



Importing data

GAStech_nodes <- read_csv("GAStech_email_node.csv")
GAStech_edges <- read_csv("GAStech_email_edge-v2.csv")



Reviewing the data

glimpse(GAStech_edges)

Columns: 8

$ SentDate    <chr> "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014", "6/1/2014"…
$ SentTime    <time> 08:39:00, 08:39:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:00, 08:58:0…

$ targetLabel <chr> "Isak.Baza", "Lucas.Alcazar", "Felix.Resumir", "Hideki.Coc…

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text:
Data Wrangling

Wrangling time
SentDate is imported as a character data type and needs to be converted to a proper date format. dmy() converts the date string and wday() extracts the day of the week — both from the lubridate package.

GAStech_edges <- GAStech_edges %>%
  mutate(SendDate = dmy(SentDate)) %>%
  mutate(Weekday = wday(SentDate,
                        label = TRUE,
                        abbr = FALSE))



Wrangling attributes
We filter to keep only work-related emails and aggregate by source, target and weekday to count the number of emails sent.

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges %>%
  filter(MainSubject == "Work related") %>%
  group_by(source, target, Weekday) %>%
  summarise(Weight = n()) %>%
  filter(source != target) %>%
  filter(Weight > 1) %>%
  ungroup()

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text:
Creating Network Objects with tidygraph

Building the tidygraph object
tbl_graph() creates a network object from the nodes and edges dataframes.

GAStech_graph <- tbl_graph(nodes = GAStech_nodes,
                            edges = GAStech_edges_aggregated,
                            directed = TRUE)

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
section: Plotting Static Network Graphs with ggraph
text:
Plotting Static Network Graphs with ggraph

Basic network graph

The plotThe code chunk














ggraph(GAStech_graph) +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point()






With theme and colour

The plotThe code chunk














g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(colour = Weekday)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 3) +
  theme_graph()
g






Modifying edges by weight

The plotThe code chunk














g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = Weight),
                 alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 3) +
  theme_graph()
g

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text:
Creating Facet Graphs

Facet by edges

The plotThe code chunk














set_graph_style()
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = Weight),
                 alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 2)
g + facet_edges(~Weekday)






Facet by nodes

The plotThe code chunk














set_graph_style()
g <- ggraph(GAStech_graph,
            layout = "nicely") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = Weight),
                 alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department),
                  size = 2)
g + facet_nodes(~Department) +
  th_foreground(foreground = "grey80",
                border = TRUE)

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
text:
Network Metrics Analysis

Computing centrality indices
centrality_betweenness() measures how often a node lies on the shortest path between other nodes — a key measure of influence.

The plotThe code chunk














g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  mutate(betweenness_centrality = centrality_betweenness()) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = Weight),
                 alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = Department,
                      size = betweenness_centrality)) +
  theme_graph()
g






Visualising community
group_edge_betweenness() detects communities — groups of nodes that are more connected to each other than to the rest of the network.

The plotThe code chunk














g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_edge_betweenness(weights = Weight,
                           directed = TRUE))) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(width = Weight),
                 alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(colour = community),
                  size = 3) +
  theme_graph()
g

title: 7a. Modelling, Visualising and Analysing Network Data with R
section: Building Interactive Network Graph with visNetwork
text:
Building Interactive Network Graph with visNetwork

Data preparation
visNetwork requires nodes to have an id column and edges to have from and to columns.

GAStech_edges_aggregated <- GAStech_edges_aggregated %>%
  rename(from = source,
         to = target)

GAStech_nodes <- GAStech_nodes %>%
  rename(id = id,
         label = label)



Basic interactive graph

The plotThe code chunk











visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr")






With visual attributes and interactivity

The plotThe code chunk











GAStech_nodes <- GAStech_nodes %>%
  rename(group = Department)

visNetwork(GAStech_nodes,
           GAStech_edges_aggregated) %>%
  visIgraphLayout(layout = "layout_with_fr") %>%
  visLegend() %>%
  visLayout(randomSeed = 123)

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text: Parallel coordinates plots are designed for comparing multiple numerical variables across many observations simultaneously. In this exercise I used the World Happiness 2018 data to explore patterns across happiness indicators by region.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text: Parallel coordinates plots are designed for comparing multiple numerical variables across many observations simultaneously. In this exercise I used the World Happiness 2018 data to explore patterns across happiness indicators by region.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Reflections
The main strength of parallel coordinates plots is that they can show many variables at once without needing separate charts. Scaling with uniminmax was important because without it, variables with different units and ranges are impossible to compare on the same plot. The facet version by region revealed clear differences in the happiness profiles of different parts of the world. The interactive parallelPlot version was more practical for analysis since dragging on any axis filters the lines and makes it easier to focus on specific subgroups.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(GGally, parallelPlot, tidyverse)



Importing data

wh <- read_csv("WHData-2018.csv")

wh <- wh |>
  mutate(Region = as.factor(Region))

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Static Parallel Coordinates Plot

Basic parallel coordinates

The plotThe code chunk














ggparcoord(data = wh, columns = c(7:12))






With boxplot and colour by region

The plotThe code chunk



ggparcoord(data = wh, 
           columns = c(7:12))











ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Parallel Coordinates Plot of World Happiness Variables")






With facet by region

The plotThe code chunk














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region)






With rotated x-axis labels

The plotThe code chunk














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text: A correlation matrix shows the pairwise correlation coefficients between all variables in a dataset. In this exercise I used the Wine Quality dataset to explore relationships between chemical properties of wine.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text: A correlation matrix shows the pairwise correlation coefficients between all variables in a dataset. In this exercise I used the Wine Quality dataset to explore relationships between chemical properties of wine.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Reflections
There are several ways to visualise a correlation matrix in R, each suited for different purposes. pairs() gives a quick scatter plot overview, ggcorrmat() is useful because it layers in statistical significance automatically, and corrplot() is the most visually polished and flexible. Reordering by hierarchical clustering was a useful technique, it groups related variables together, making underlying patterns much easier to read. The significance test overlay also helped distinguish meaningful correlations from noise.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(corrplot, ggstatsplot, tidyverse)



Importing data

wine <- read_csv("wine_quality.csv")

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Building Correlation Matrix — pairs() method

Basic correlation matrix

The plotThe code chunk














pairs(wine[,1:11])






Lower corner only

The plotThe code chunk














pairs(wine[,2:12], upper.panel = NULL)






Upper corner only

The plotThe code chunk














pairs(wine[,2:12], lower.panel = NULL)






With correlation coefficients

The plotThe code chunk














panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits=2, prefix="", cex.cor, ...) {
  usr <- par("usr")
  on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y, use="complete.obs"))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits=digits)[1]
  txt <- paste(prefix, txt, sep="")
  if(missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8/strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * (1 + r) / 2)
}
pairs(wine[,2:12], upper.panel = panel.cor)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text: A funnel plot is used for fair comparison between groups of different sizes. In this exercise I used COVID-19 data from Jakarta to compare cumulative fatality rates across sub-districts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text: A funnel plot is used for fair comparison between groups of different sizes. In this exercise I used COVID-19 data from Jakarta to compare cumulative fatality rates across sub-districts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Reflections
Funnel plots were completely new to me. The key insight is that comparing raw rates between areas of different sizes is unfair, a small sub-district with 2 deaths out of 10 cases looks much worse than a large one with 200 deaths out of 5000 cases, even if the true rate is similar.
The funnel shape comes from the fact that uncertainty decreases as sample size grows, small areas sit further out in the funnel, and outliers outside the 95% or 99% limits are the ones worth investigating.
Building the ggplot2 version from scratch was challenging but helped me understand exactly how the confidence limits are calculated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(tidyverse, FunnelPlotR, plotly, knitr)



Importing data

covid19 <- read_csv("COVID-19_DKI_Jakarta.csv") %>%
  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
FunnelPlotR methods

Basic funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk











A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 0 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 





funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Positive,
  denominator = Death,
  group = `Sub-district`
)






Makeover 1 — using proportions

The plotThe code chunk











A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 





funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group = `Sub-district`,
  data_type = "PR",
  xrange = c(0, 6500),
  yrange = c(0, 0.05)
)






Makeover 2 — with proper labels

The plotThe code chunk











A funnel plot object with 267 points of which 7 are outliers. 
Plot is adjusted for overdispersion. 





funnel_plot(
  .data = covid19,
  numerator = Death,
  denominator = Positive,
  group = `Sub-district`,
  data_type = "PR",
  xrange = c(0, 6500),
  yrange = c(0, 0.05),
  label = NA,
  title = "Cumulative COVID-19 Fatality Rate by Cumulative Total Number of COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  x_label = "Cumulative COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  y_label = "Cumulative Fatality Rate"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Funnel Plot using ggplot2

Computing derived fields

df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  mutate(rate.se = sqrt((rate*(1-rate)) / (Positive))) %>%
  filter(rate > 0)

fit.mean <- weighted.mean(df$rate, 1/df$rate.se^2)



Calculate 95% and 99.9% confidence limits

number.seq <- seq(1, max(df$Positive), 1)
number.ll95 <- fit.mean - 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ul95 <- fit.mean + 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ll999 <- fit.mean - 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ul999 <- fit.mean + 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
dfCI <- data.frame(number.ll95, number.ul95,
                   number.ll999, number.ul999,
                   number.seq, fit.mean)



Static funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk














p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate)) +
  geom_point(aes(label=`Sub-district`),
             alpha=0.4,
             size=0.9) +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ll95),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40",
            linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ul95),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40",
            linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ll999),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ul999),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40") +
  geom_hline(data = dfCI,
             aes(yintercept = fit.mean),
             linewidth = 0.4,
             colour = "grey40") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim=c(0,0.05)) +
  ggtitle("Cumulative Fatality Rate by Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  xlab("Cumulative Number of COVID-19 Cases") +
  ylab("Cumulative Fatality Rate") +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size=12))
p






Interactive funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk











fp_ggplotly <- ggplotly(p,
  tooltip = c("label",
              "x",
              "y"))
fp_ggplotly

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
ggstatsplot is really useful because it combines the chart and the statistical test result in one — something that would normally take two separate steps in R. The ggbetweenstats() function was the most interesting since it automatically runs the right test depending on the number of groups and shows the pairwise comparisons directly on the chart.
The Bayes Factor in gghistostats() was new to me — it gives a more intuitive way to interpret evidence compared to just a p-value.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated graphics can make data stories more engaging and memorable compared to static charts. In this exercise, I learnt how to create animated visualisations using gganimate and plotly, while also practising data wrangling with tidyr and dplyr.
Before diving in, it’s worth asking, does animation actually add value here? For exploratory analysis, probably not. But for presentations, a well-placed animation can really help the audience connect with the data.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Reflections
This was my first time making charts interactive in R. The ggiraph package was surprisingly straightforward — just swap geom_point() for geom_point_interactive() and wrap it in girafe().
The most interesting part was the coordinated multiple views where hovering on one chart highlights the same student in another chart. This is really useful for spotting patterns across different variables at the same time.
I found plotly easier to use than ggiraph since ggplotly() can just wrap an existing ggplot chart. The crosstalk + DT combination was also very cool, being able to filter a chart by clicking on a table row is something I can see being very useful in real dashboards.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing data
This exercise uses a different dataset — GlobalPopulation.xls instead of Exam_data.csv.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation — gganimate

Static bubble plot first
Always good to build the static version before animating — helps check the layout looks right.

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young')






Animated bubble plot
Adding transition_time() animates the chart across years. ease_aes('linear') makes the movement smooth and consistent.

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') +
  transition_time(Year) +       
  ease_aes('linear')

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation — plotly

Using ggplotly()
This converts the ggplot animation into an interactive plotly chart with a play/pause button and a year slider — much more user-friendly for presentations.

The plotThe code chunk











gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
       aes(x = Old, 
           y = Young, 
           size = Population, 
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') + 
  theme(legend.position='none')

ggplotly(gg)






Using plot_ly()
Building the animated chart directly with plot_ly() — slightly different syntax but gives more control over the output.

The plotThe code chunk











bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Old, 
          y = ~Young, 
          size = ~Population, 
          color = ~Continent,
          sizes = c(2, 100),
          frame = ~Year, 
          text = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type = 'scatter',
          mode = 'markers') %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)
bp

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text: In this exercise, I explored ggplot2 extensions that go beyond basic charts. I learned how ggrepel prevents overlapping text labels, making scatter plots much cleaner and easier to read. I also experimented with ggthemes and hrbrthemes to apply professional styling, and used patchwork to combine multiple plots into a single layout. These tools showed me that good data visualisation is as much about presentation as it is about the data itself.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel
One of the challenges when annotating scatter plots is that labels often overlap each other. The ggrepel package solves this by automatically adjusting label positions.

Without ggrepel (messy labels)

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")






With ggrepel (clean labels)

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

Beyond ggplot2 Themes
The ggthemes and hrbrthemes packages provide ready-made professional themes for ggplot2 charts.

Default theme_gray()

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")






Using ggthemes: theme_economist()

The plotThe code

       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +

Using hrbrthemes: theme_ipsum()

The plotThe code

       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()






theme_ipsum() with custom settings

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25",

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond Single Graph
The patchwork package makes it easy to combine multiple ggplot2 charts into one layout.

Creating the individual plots

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")



Combining two graphs

The plotThe code














p1 + p2






Combining three graphs

The plotThe code














(p1 / p2) | p3






With tags

The plotThe code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')






With inset

The plotThe code














p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)






patchwork + ggtheme combined

The plotThe code














patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Extra Alternatives

Alternative 1: Using theme_wsj()
I used theme_wsj() instead of theme_economist() for a cleaner, more minimal Wall Street Journal style.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_wsj()






Alternative 2: Using theme_fivethirtyeight()
This theme mimics the style used by FiveThirtyEight, great for clean data journalism visuals.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()






Alternative 3: Different patchwork layout
Instead of stacking p1/p2 on the left, I place p1 on top spanning full width for better visual hierarchy.

The plotThe code














p1 / (p2 | p3)






Alternative 4: Letter tags instead of Roman numerals
Using letter tags (A, B, C) makes subplots easier to reference in written explanations.

The plotThe code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'A')






Alternative 5: Inset in different position
Placing the inset in the bottom right instead of top left avoids covering the main data cluster.

The plotThe code














p3 + inset_element(p2,
                   left = 0.5,
                   bottom = 0.0,
                   right = 1.0,
                   top = 0.4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text: In this exercise, I was introduced to ggplot2 and the layered grammar of graphics. I learned that every chart in R is built by adding layers — starting from data, then aesthetics, then geometry. I explored many different chart types including histograms, dot plots, box plots, violin plots, and scatter plots. I also discovered how facets allow us to split charts by categories, and how built-in themes like theme_classic() and theme_minimal() can instantly change the look of a chart. This exercise showed me that ggplot2 is both systematic and flexible.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this exercise, I explored how to make static ggplot2 charts interactive using the ggiraph and plotly packages. Key techniques include:

Adding tooltips to display information on hover
Using data_id to highlight related elements together
Creating onclick effects to link charts to external pages
Building coordinated multiple views where interactions on one chart affect another
Using DT to create searchable, interactive data tables
Using crosstalk to link a chart and a table together




Interactivity makes charts more useful for exploration — users can investigate specific data points without needing a separate table or annotation.




Understanding the difference between tooltip, data_id and onclick arguments in ggiraph
Setting up coordinated views required linking plots through a shared highlight_key()

text:
Getting Started

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Interactive Data Visualisation — ggiraph methods

Tooltip effect
Hovering over a data point shows the student’s ID.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)






Displaying multiple information on tooltip
Hovering shows both the student’s ID and Class.

The plotThe code chunk











exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)






Customising tooltip style
Using CSS to change tooltip background and font colour.

The plotThe code chunk











tooltip_css <- "background-color:white;
font-style:bold; color:black;"

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(
      css = tooltip_css))
)






Displaying statistics on tooltip
Tooltip shows the mean Maths score with confidence interval per race.

The plotThe code chunk











tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)






Hover effect with data_id
Elements of the same CLASS are highlighted together on mouse over.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),             
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618                      
)






Styling hover effect
Custom CSS changes the highlight colour and dims non-hovered elements.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)






Combining tooltip and hover effect

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)






Click effect with onclick
Clicking a data point opens the MOE school finder page.

The plotThe code chunk











exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618)






Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
Hovering highlights the same student across both plots simultaneously.

The plotThe code chunk











p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2), 
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
         )
       )

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text: In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using ggridges and ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text: In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using ggridges and ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Reflections
This exercise introduced two new ways to visualise distribution. Ridgeline plots are great for comparing distributions across many groups at once, much cleaner than stacking multiple histograms.
The raincloud plot was the most interesting, it combines three views in one chart: the density shape, the boxplot summary, and the actual data points. This gives a much more complete picture than just a boxplot alone.
The step-by-step build up of the raincloud plot (half eye → boxplot → dots → flip) was a good way to understand how each layer adds information.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot
A ridgeline plot (also called a Joyplot) reveals the distribution of a numeric value for several groups. It works best when there are 5 or more groups with a clear pattern or ranking.

Basic ridgeline plot

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






Varying fill colours along the x axis

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Temp. [F]",
                       option = "C") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






Mapping probabilities directly onto colour

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5-stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
                      calc_ecdf = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Tail probability",
                       direction = -1) +
  theme_ridges()






Ridgeline plots with quantile lines

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  theme_ridges()






Ridgeline plot with tail probability cut points

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
    ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot
A raincloud plot combines a half-density plot, a boxplot and a dot plot — giving a much richer picture of the data distribution than a boxplot alone.

Step 1 — Half Eye graph

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA)






Step 2 — Adding boxplot with geom_boxplot()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA)






Step 3 — Adding dot plots stat_dots()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2)






Step 4 — Final raincloud plot

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text: Visualising uncertainty is about showing not just the point estimate (like a mean) but also how confident we are in that estimate. In this exercise I learnt to plot error bars, confidence intervals and uncertainty using ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text: Visualising uncertainty is about showing not just the point estimate (like a mean) but also how confident we are in that estimate. In this exercise I learnt to plot error bars, confidence intervals and uncertainty using ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Reflections
The difference between standard error bars and confidence intervals was much clearer after seeing them plotted side by side. The 95% CI version (mean ± 1.96 × SE) is more informative than just showing SE.
The interactive version combining plotly and DT was the most practical, being able to hover over a point and see the exact confidence interval values, and then filter the table at the same time, is very useful for presentations.
stat_gradientinterval() from ggdist was visually striking — the gradient effect gives a more intuitive feel for where the uncertainty is concentrated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, crosstalk, DT,
               ggdist, ggridges, colorspace,
               gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



Computing summary statistics

my_sum <- exam %>%
  group_by(RACE) %>%
  summarise(
    n=n(),
    mean=mean(MATHS),
    sd=sd(MATHS)
    ) %>%
  mutate(se=sd/sqrt(n-1))

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Visualising Uncertainty — ggplot2 methods

Standard error bars

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=RACE,
        ymin=mean-se,
        ymax=mean+se),
    width=0.2,
    colour="black",
    alpha=0.9,
    linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat="identity",
             color="red",
             size = 1.5,
             alpha=1) +
  ggtitle("Standard error of mean maths score by race")






95% Confidence interval

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
        ymin=mean-1.96*se,
        ymax=mean+1.96*se),
    width=0.2,
    colour="black",
    alpha=0.9,
    linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat="identity",
             color="red",
             size = 1.5,
             alpha=1) +
  labs(x = "Maths score",
       title = "95% confidence interval of mean maths score by race")






Interactive error bars — 99% CI

The plotThe code chunk





















shared_df = SharedData$new(my_sum)

bscols(widths = c(4,8),
       ggplotly((ggplot(shared_df) +
                   geom_errorbar(aes(
                     x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
                     ymin=mean-2.58*se,
                     ymax=mean+2.58*se),
                     width=0.2,
                     colour="black",
                     alpha=0.9,
                     size=0.5) +
                   geom_point(aes(
                     x=RACE,
                     y=mean,
                     text = paste("Race:", `RACE`,
                                  "<br>N:", `n`,
                                  "<br>Avg. Scores:", round(mean, digits = 2),
                                  "<br>95% CI:[",
                                  round((mean-2.58*se), digits = 2), ",",
                                  round((mean+2.58*se), digits = 2),"]")),
                     stat="identity",
                     color="red",
                     size = 1.5,
                     alpha=1) +
                   xlab("Race") +
                   ylab("Average Scores") +
                   theme_minimal() +
                   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(
                     angle = 45, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1)) +
                   ggtitle("99% Confidence interval of average maths scores by race")),
                tooltip = "text"),
       DT::datatable(shared_df,
                     rownames = FALSE,
                     class="compact",
                     width="100%",
                     options = list(pageLength = 10,
                                    scrollX=T),
                     colnames = c("No. of pupils",
                                  "Avg Scores",
                                  "Std Dev",
                                  "Std Error")) %>%
         formatRound(columns=c('mean', 'sd', 'se'),
                     digits=2))

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text: Ternary plots display the distribution of three-part compositional data. Each side of the triangle represents one of three components. In this exercise I used Singapore population data to explore the proportion of young, economically active and old residents by planning area.

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text: Ternary plots display the distribution of three-part compositional data. Each side of the triangle represents one of three components. In this exercise I used Singapore population data to explore the proportion of young, economically active and old residents by planning area.

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text:
Reflections
A ternary plot is useful when three variables always sum to a fixed total, something a standard scatter plot cannot show. The key takeaway is that the position of each point encodes all three proportions simultaneously, making it easy to spot which planning areas are ageing faster than others. The interactive plotly version added practical value since hovering reveals the exact planning area behind each point.

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse)



Importing data

Preparing the data

agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  mutate(`Year` = as.character(Year)) %>%
  spread(AG, Population) %>%
  mutate(YOUNG = rowSums(.[4:8])) %>%
  mutate(ACTIVE = rowSums(.[9:16])) %>%
  mutate(OLD = rowSums(.[17:21])) %>%
  mutate(TOTAL = rowSums(.[22:24])) %>%
  filter(Year == "2018") %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)

       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +

  theme_rgbw()






Interactive ternary plot using plotly

The plotThe code chunk











label <- function(txt) {
  list(
    text = txt, x = 0.1, y = 1,
    ax = 0, ay = 0,
    xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
    align = "center",
    font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"),
    bgcolor = "#b3b3b3", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 2
  )
}
axis <- function(txt) {
  list(title = txt, tickformat = ".0%", tickfont = list(size = 10))
}
ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = axis("Young"),
  baxis = axis("Active"),
  caxis = axis("Old")
)
plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated,
  a = ~YOUNG, b = ~ACTIVE, c = ~OLD,
  color = I("black"),
  type = "scatterternary"
) |>
  layout(annotations = label("Ternary Markers"),
         ternary = ternaryAxes)

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Reflections
The main takeaway is that normalising the data before plotting is essential. Without it, variables with larger absolute values dominate the colour scale and make other variables impossible to compare. The heatmaply interactive version adds real value over the static version because you can hover over individual cells to read exact values and zoom into specific regions. Clustering the rows and columns together also helped reveal groups of countries with similar happiness profiles.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text: Treemaps display hierarchical data using nested rectangles where the size of each rectangle represents a quantitative value. In this exercise I used Singapore private property transaction data from 2018.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text: Treemaps display hierarchical data using nested rectangles where the size of each rectangle represents a quantitative value. In this exercise I used Singapore private property transaction data from 2018.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Reflections
Treemaps are effective when you need to show both hierarchy and proportion in one chart. The size encoding shows total units sold at a glance, while the colour encoding adds a second layer of information on median unit price. The treemapify approach using ggplot2 syntax was more intuitive since the layering logic is familiar. The data wrangling step using group_by() and summarise() before plotting was a good reminder that most real-world visualisation work starts with data preparation.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R

Getting Started

Importing data

                          `Total Unit Sold` = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Total Area` = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),

realis2018_summarised <- realis2018 %>%

            `Total Area` = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text: Text visualisation transforms unstructured text data into visual representations to support understanding, grouping, comparison and correlation of textual content. In this exercise, the tidytext framework is used to process and visualise newsgroup messages from the 20 Newsgroups dataset, covering techniques such as word clouds, TF-IDF analysis and bigram network graphs.

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text: Text visualisation transforms unstructured text data into visual representations to support understanding, grouping, comparison and correlation of textual content. In this exercise, the tidytext framework is used to process and visualise newsgroup messages from the 20 Newsgroups dataset, covering techniques such as word clouds, TF-IDF analysis and bigram network graphs.

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
Reflections
The key takeaway from this exercise is that text data requires significant preprocessing before meaningful visualisation is possible — removing headers, signatures and stop words is just as important as the visualisation itself. TF-IDF is particularly useful because it surfaces words that are distinctive to a newsgroup rather than just frequent across all documents. Visualising bigrams as a network graph was an effective way to reveal common two-word phrases and their relationships.

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(tidytext, widyr, wordcloud, DT, 
               ggwordcloud, textplot, lubridate, hms,
               tidyverse, tidygraph, ggraph, igraph)

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
section: Importing Multiple Text Files from Multiple Folders
text:
Importing Multiple Text Files from Multiple Folders

Creating a folder list

news20 <- "20news/"



Defining a function to read files
A custom function read_folder() is written to read all text files from a folder into a single data frame. map() of the purrr package is used to apply the function to each file, while unnest() flattens the resulting list into a data frame.

read_folder <- function(infolder) {
  tibble(file = dir(infolder,
                    full.names = TRUE)) %>%
    mutate(text = map(file,
                      read_lines)) %>%
    transmute(id = basename(file),
              text) %>%
    unnest(text)
}



Reading in all messages from the 20news folder

raw_text <- tibble(folder =
                     dir(news20,
                         full.names = TRUE)) %>%
  mutate(folder_out = map(folder,
                          read_folder)) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(folder_out)) %>%
  mutate(newsgroup = basename(folder)) %>%
  select(newsgroup, id, text)

write_rds(raw_text, "20news.rds")

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
Initial EDA
The bar chart below shows the frequency of messages by newsgroup.

The plotThe code chunk














raw_text %>%
  group_by(newsgroup) %>%
  summarize(messages = n_distinct(id)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(messages, newsgroup)) +
  geom_col(fill = "lightblue") +
  labs(y = NULL)

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
Introducing tidytext

Removing headers and automated email signatures
Each message contains a header and automated email signature that are not useful for analysis. cumsum() of base R is used to return a cumulative sum, while str_detect() of stringr detects the presence of a pattern in a string.

cleaned_text <- raw_text %>%
  group_by(newsgroup, id) %>%
  filter(cumsum(text == "") > 0,
         cumsum(str_detect(
           text, "^--")) == 0) %>%
  ungroup()



Removing lines with nested quotes
Regular expressions are used to remove lines with nested text representing quotes from other users.

cleaned_text <- cleaned_text %>%
  filter(str_detect(text, "^[^>]+[A-Za-z\\d]")
         | text == "",
         !str_detect(text,
                     "writes(:|\\.\\.\\.)$"),
         !str_detect(text,
                     "^In article <")
  )



Text data processing
unnest_tokens() of tidytext splits each message into individual words (tokens). Stop words — common words like “the” and “and” — are then removed using stop_words.

usenet_words <- cleaned_text %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text) %>%
  filter(str_detect(word, "[a-z']$"),
         !word %in% stop_words$word)

Counting individual words across the entire dataset:

usenet_words %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

# A tibble: 5,542 × 2
   word           n
   <chr>      <int>
 1 people        57
 2 time          50
 3 jesus         47
 4 god           44
 5 message       40
 6 br            27
 7 bible         23
 8 drive         23
 9 homosexual    23
10 read          22
# ℹ 5,532 more rows


Counting words within each newsgroup:

words_by_newsgroup <- usenet_words %>%
  count(newsgroup, word, sort = TRUE) %>%
  ungroup()



Visualising words in newsgroups — wordcloud
wordcloud() of the wordcloud package is used to plot a static wordcloud.

The plotThe code chunk














wordcloud(words_by_newsgroup$word,
          words_by_newsgroup$n,
          max.words = 300)






Visualising words in newsgroups — ggwordcloud
geom_text_wordcloud() of ggwordcloud is used to plot a wordcloud faceted by newsgroup.

The plotThe code chunk

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
TF-IDF Analysis

Computing TF-IDF within newsgroups
bind_tf_idf() of tidytext computes and binds the term frequency, inverse document frequency and TF-IDF of a tidy text dataset.

tf_idf <- words_by_newsgroup %>%
  bind_tf_idf(word, newsgroup, n) %>%
  arrange(desc(tf_idf))



Visualising TF-IDF as interactive table
datatable() of DT creates an interactive table with pagination and filtering. formatRound() customises the decimal places and formatStyle() adjusts the row height.

The plotThe code chunk











DT::datatable(tf_idf, filter = 'top') %>%
  formatRound(columns = c('tf', 'idf',
                          'tf_idf'),
              digits = 3) %>%
  formatStyle(0,
              target = 'row',
              lineHeight='25%')






Visualising TF-IDF within newsgroups

The plotThe code chunk














tf_idf %>%
  filter(str_detect(newsgroup, "^sci\\.")) %>%
  group_by(newsgroup) %>%
  slice_max(tf_idf, n = 12) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(word = reorder(word, tf_idf)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(tf_idf, word, fill = newsgroup)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ newsgroup, scales = "free") +
  labs(x = "tf-idf", y = NULL)

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
Word Correlation Analysis

Counting and correlating pairs of words
pairwise_cor() of widyr is used to find words that are correlated with each other within the same newsgroup.

newsgroup_cors <- words_by_newsgroup %>%
  pairwise_cor(newsgroup,
               word,
               n,
               sort = TRUE)



Visualising correlation as a network

The plotThe code chunk














set.seed(2017)

newsgroup_cors %>%
  filter(correlation > .025) %>%
  graph_from_data_frame() %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(alpha = correlation,
                     width = correlation)) +
  geom_node_point(size = 6,
                  color = "lightblue") +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name),
                 repel = TRUE) +
  theme_graph()

title: 7b. Visualising and Analysing Text Data with R
text:
Bigram Analysis

Counting bigrams
A bigram is a pair of consecutive words. unnest_tokens() with token = "ngrams" and n = 2 extracts all bigrams from the text.

bigrams <- cleaned_text %>%
  unnest_tokens(bigram,
                text,
                token = "ngrams",
                n = 2)

bigrams_count <- bigrams %>%
  count(bigram, sort = TRUE)



Cleaning bigrams
Bigrams containing stop words are removed by separating each bigram into two words and filtering out those containing stop words.

bigrams_separated <- bigrams_count %>%
  separate(bigram, c("word1", "word2"),
           sep = " ")

bigrams_filtered <- bigrams_separated %>%
  filter(!word1 %in% stop_words$word) %>%
  filter(!word2 %in% stop_words$word)



Counting bigrams again
unite() of tidyr is used to combine the two word columns back into a single bigram column.

bigrams_united <- bigrams_filtered %>%
  unite(bigram, word1, word2, sep = " ")

bigrams_united

Creating a network graph from bigram data
graph_from_data_frame() of igraph converts the bigram data into a network object.

bigram_graph <- bigrams_filtered %>%
  filter(n > 3) %>%
  filter(!is.na(word1), !is.na(word2)) %>%
  graph_from_data_frame()

Visualising bigrams with ggraph

The plotThe code chunk














set.seed(1234)

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link() +
  geom_node_point() +
  geom_node_text(aes(label = name),
                 vjust = 1,
                 hjust = 1)






Revised bigram network graph

The plotThe code chunk

                 length = unit(.15, "inches"))

ggraph(bigram_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(aes(edge_alpha = n),
                 show.legend = FALSE,
                 arrow = a,
                 end_cap = circle(.07, 'inches')) +
